text: This video demonstrates the technique of laparoscopic salvage lateral pelvic lymph node dissection for local recurrence developing at the lateral pelvic lymph nodes after rectal cancer surgery.
The surgeon carefully dissects adhesions of the small intestine within the pelvic cavity. The right ureter is isolated, dissected, and displaced toward the left side.
The surgeon continues dissection at the vesicoureteral junction, revealing fibrosis at the outer side of the pelvic plexus. The external iliac artery and vein are exposed during the procedure.

The surgeon exposes the psoas major muscle and performs dissection along its surface. The internal obturator muscle is also exposed. A small vessel is carefully divided using an ultrasonically activated scalpel.

The surgeon dissects tissue along the vesico-hypogastric fascia, continuing dissection along the surface of the internal obturator muscle. The obturator nerve is identified and carefully preserved during the procedure.
The surgeon exposes the sacral plexus and divides the distal side of the obturator vessels, followed by division of the proximal side of these vessels.
The surgeon divides the proximal side of the umbilical artery, followed by the division of its distal side. Subsequently, the distal side of the inferior vesicle artery is divided.

The surgeon exposes the levator ani muscle and the main trunk of the internal iliac artery. The proximal side of the superior vesicle artery is divided, followed by the division of the proximal side of the internal iliac artery.

The surgeon exposes the surface of the bladder and divides the distal side of the inferior vesicle vein, followed by division of its proximal side. The surface of the internal iliac vein is then exposed.

The surgeon divides the proximal side of another inferior vesicle vein, followed by the division of the distal side of the internal pudendo artery and the distal side of the inferior gluteal artery. The surface of the internal iliac vein is fully exposed.
The surgeon dissects tissue from the pelvic plexus, dividing the distal side of the inferior vesicle artery and subsequently dividing the distal side of another inferior vesicle artery.

The surgeon completes the dissection, having exposed and divided multiple arteries and veins in the pelvic region, including the inferior vesicle artery and vein, internal iliac artery, and umbilical artery.
